source.title: A Tutorial on the Cross-Entropy Method

confidence_rationale: The tutorial is the standard peer-reviewed account of CE/CEM as elite-set refitting of a sampling distribution. It directly explains why final CEM candidates can lose support: the method is designed to concentrate probability mass around high-performing elites. The mapping to REE is algorithmic rather than biological, so it supports the diagnosis and instrumentation need more strongly than any particular repair.
failure_signatures: If the elite set contains only one first-action class, ordinary CEM refit can concentrate the next proposal distribution on that class and erase downstream choice support.
A support-preserving repair that changes the score function rather than the proposal distribution would not address the collapse mode implied by elite-set refitting.

mapping.source_claim_statement: The CE method uses randomized samples, selects elite samples by objective value, and updates the sampling distribution toward those elites.
mapping.ree_translation: V3's CEM collapse after V3-EXQ-563 is not surprising: if the elite set is one first-action class, the next hippocampal proposal distribution is mathematically pushed toward that class. ARC-065 therefore needs candidate-support diagnostics and, experimentally, a proposal-side support-preservation option before E3 score biases can be interpreted.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper does not discuss REE, hippocampal rollout, discrete first-action classes, or action-object decoding. The translation is from generic CEM mechanics to REE's CEM-style trajectory proposer.
mapping.source_context: Peer-reviewed operations-research tutorial covering CE/CEM for combinatorial optimization, multi-extremal optimization, rare-event simulation, and machine learning.

confidence_components.notes: High-quality source for CEM mechanics. Mapping is local and algorithmic: it grounds why elite-refit can collapse support, not what biological hippocampus does.
